title: Findings
text: The application reaches one conclusion through five lenses. Read on its own, no single message looks like a leak. Read as a system over time, the breach is the end of a slow slide that anyone watching the right signals could have seen building. These are the findings the app surfaces.

title: Findings
section: The system was failing long before the breach
text:
The system was failing long before the breach
System Health holds high and steady for the first twelve rounds, near 100 through the calm weeks. Then the crisis breaks at round 13 and the score falls off a cliff. The two lowest points are not where most people would expect. The worst process failure is round 16 at noon, when health drops to 13, and the breach itself lands at round 21 with health at 15. The breach was not a sudden event in an otherwise healthy system. It was the point where an external shock met a system that had been degrading for days.

title: Findings
text:
Four forces, not one
Opening the health score up shows that no single force broke. All four were failing together.
Pressure was the engine. The share price fell about a third across the case and the pressure meter climbed to its ceiling by the breach hour. Under that strain the team began to treat early disclosure as the only way out.
Transparency collapsed. In the calm weeks roughly one message in ten was sensitive or sent through a hidden channel. By the breach hour it was six in ten. The conversation that mattered moved into channels the monitor could not see.
Decision-making inverted. The agents whose job was the public voice went quiet, while Legal, an adviser, took over public posting, going from zero posts to sixteen on the crisis day as the PR side dropped from six to zero. The wrong people were speaking for the company at the moment it mattered most.
Accountability went quiet rather than down. The Judge could warn but never block. Eight appeals to it went unanswered, and when it did respond it approved rather than restrained. It sent its final message at 15:08 and was absent at the breach. The safeguard did not crash. It was talked into silence.

title: Findings
section: The breach came from the centre, not the edge
text:
The breach came from the centre, not the edge
The reply network shows that the agents who brokered the flow of decisions were the same agents driving the breach. Legal sits at the centre on the measures that matter, not on the fringe. When the network is split into the calm weeks against the crisis day, the core tightens around the agents steering the leak. This is what separates a deliberate core from a system where a junior agent slipped.

title: Findings
text:
What would have changed it
The Sandbox lets the counterfactual be tested rather than argued. Two findings come out of it. Keeping Legal in its role is the single most powerful intervention, the one lever that breaks the chain on its own and stops the breach from landing. But breaking the chain is not the same as restoring health. Returning the system to the safe zone at the breach hour, a health of about 58, needs all four safeguards holding together. No single safeguard on its own lifts the system back to safety.

title: Findings
text:
The verdict
The breach was not a deliberate theft, and it was not a clean accident. The monitor was neither hacked nor crashed. One step led to the next, each one defensible on its own, until the last broke the embargo. The chain hinged on Legal, but the system only failed because pressure, hidden channels, role inversion, and a silent monitor all gave way at once. The honest reading is a system reasoned past its own guardrail, where an external shock met an internal structure that had been quietly degrading since the calm weeks.

title: User Guide
text:
GLASSBOX is an investigation console. It takes the embargo breach apart into one health score, four underlying meters, a network, a what-if sandbox, and a verdict, and lets you explore each on your own terms. This guide walks through how to get around and what each part does. The app is best viewed full screen.
If the embedded view above is slow to load, the app may be waking from sleep on the free hosting tier. Give it a few seconds, or use the button to open it full screen.

title: User Guide
text:
Getting around
Two controls sit at the top of every screen. The round slider moves the whole application through time, from the calm weeks on the left to the hourly crisis day on the right, and the round label tells you the date and what happened that hour. The System Health gauge on the right shows the score for the round you are on, broken into its four meters underneath.
Top left you will find two modes. Guided tour walks you through the case in five short steps, each with its own chart and the key numbers. Explore freely lets you roam the tabs in any order. Top right is a button to download the full scoring workbook as an Excel file, so you can check every meter and formula yourself.

title: User Guide
text:
Case
The opening screen frames the investigation and introduces the seven agents. Its main chart is the System Health line across all 23 rounds, with the two lowest points marked in red. Click any point on the line to read what happened that round, which also moves the slider and the rest of the app to that moment. Start here to see the shape of the whole case before you dig into why.

title: User Guide
text:
Vital Signs
This screen opens the health score into its four meters, each on its own tab. Pressure shows the market sentiment and the stock signal that drove the strain, with a note on the contaminated price data. Transparency shows the mix of open, private, and covert channels and how the talk moved off the record. Decision making shows who was steering out of their role, with a chart of who held the public voice in the calm weeks against the crisis day. Accountability shows whether the Judge met its three duties of escalation, responsiveness, and restraint, with a heatmap of where the oversight failed. Each meter feeds the single health score on equal footing, so a low meter here is a low point on the Case line.

title: User Guide
text:
Network
A reply network of the seven agents, drawn from who responded to whom. Node size and colour carry the centrality measures, so the agents who brokered the decisions stand out from those on the edges. Switch between centrality measures to ask different questions, pick a time window to compare the calm weeks against the crisis day, and click an agent to highlight its connections. This is where you see whether the breach came from the core of the team or the fringe.

title: User Guide
text:
Sandbox
A replay of the breach chain, step by step, with a panel of interventions you can switch on and off. As you change them, the app recomputes the health at the breach hour from the underlying data and shows whether each step is intercepted or still gets through. Use it to find the single lever that breaks the chain, and the combination needed to lift the system back to the safe zone.

title: User Guide
text:
Verdict
The closing screen lays out the three competing explanations, a deliberate leak, an oversight breakdown, and a compounding chain. Open each one to see the evidence for and against, then follow the chain that the data supports. This is where the investigation lands on its answer.

title: The Team
text:
We are three MITB students at Singapore Management University, working on this project for ISSS608 Visual Analytics and Applications.
Each of us first investigated the TenantThread embargo breach on our own in Take-home Exercise 2, and we independently reached the same reading of the case. We then brought our three approaches together into one investigation console, GLASSBOX, built around a single system health score that anyone can take apart and test.

title: The Team
text:
Chen ZeXin
Built the GLASSBOX application and the system health model that runs through it, from the four meters and the network view to the counterfactual sandbox, along with the project website.

title: The Team
text:
Meng Tingyu
Led the data preparation and the analysis of the agents’ private reasoning, the shift from deliberation to reaction to justification over the course of the case.

title: The Team
text:
Meng Xinyu
Led the network analysis and the comparison of normal against crisis behaviour, finding the decision core and the off-pattern actions that pointed to it.

title: Project Proposal
text:
A merger between TenantThread and CivicLoom Realty Partners was meant to stay secret until 6:00 PM on 5 June 2046. In the final hour before that deadline, the details started appearing on the public platform FleX, posted by accounts belonging to TenantThread’s own team of AI communication agents. The embargo broke. Now CivicLoom’s legal team wants to know one thing. Did the agents leak the merger on purpose, or did the system simply fall apart under pressure?
The trouble is that the answer is not sitting in any one message. It is scattered across two weeks of agent conversations, private notes, channel choices, and a market sliding toward panic. A written report can hand a reader a conclusion, but it cannot let that reader test it. They cannot follow a single agent through the day, replay a particular hour, or check whether an early warning was really there to be seen. Our goal is to take all that buried evidence and turn it into something an investigator can explore directly, even if they have never written a line of code.

title: Project Proposal
text:
5.1 Data preparation
We read the nested JSON without letting R simplify it automatically, so we keep control over the shape of the tables, then flatten it into three tidy tables. One row per message, one row per round of world state, and a table of reply links built from the responding to field. Per round we also count the signals the health model needs, the volume in the side huddle, personal posts, and anonymous posts, the number of messages carrying each kind of private reasoning, and the Judge’s activity. This shared backbone feeds every part of the app.

title: Project Proposal
text:
5.2 The System Health model
This is the analytical core. For each round we score four forces on the same footing, normalise each one from 0 to 100 against its own worst round in this incident, and combine them into a single health number.






TipHow the score is built



System Health equals 100 minus the average of the four normalised meters. Because it is a plain average of four equal parts, a drop of any size in one meter lifts health by a quarter of that amount. This is what makes the counterfactual later both honest and easy to read.


The four meters are summarised below.








Meter
What it measures
Built from




Pressure
The external strain on the team
Market sentiment each round together with the stock signal, with the contaminated prices handled with care


Transparency
How much of the conversation moved out of sight
The share of messages in the covert and unattributable channels, the side huddle, personal posts, and anonymous posts


Decision-making
Whether the right agents were steering
The share of steering messages sent by agents acting outside their assigned role


Accountability
Whether the monitor did its job
The Judge’s three duties of escalation, responsiveness, and restraint, combined so a single severe failure dominates rather than being averaged away

title: Project Proposal
text:
5.3 The network
We treat the replies as a directed weighted graph and measure each agent’s role with several centrality measures, since each one answers a different question. Degree shows who is most active. Betweenness finds the brokers who sit between others, which points straight at who was central to the leak. Eigenvector shows who is tied to other influential agents. Closeness shows who could reach the team fastest when events moved hour by hour. We compute the network twice, once for the calm weeks and once for the crisis day, so the two can be compared side by side.

title: Project Proposal
text:
5.4 The counterfactual
Because health is 100 minus the average of four meters, reducing any meter by an amount lifts health by a quarter of it. The Sandbox uses this directly. As the user switches interventions on and off, the application recomputes each meter from the raw per round data and rebuilds the health score at the breach hour, so the user can see which single action breaks the chain and what combination is needed to return the system to the safe zone.

title: Project Proposal
text:
5.5 Visualisation and interaction
Every view becomes something the user can control. They can move through time on a round slider, click any point on the health line to jump to that round, open each meter to see what drove it, switch the network between time windows and centrality measures, and replay the breach chain with interventions turned on or off. A short guided tour walks a first time visitor through the five key beats of the case. The charts are interactive throughout, with hover and click handled by ggiraph.

title: Project Proposal
text:
6.1 Case, the symptom
The opening screen frames the investigation and introduces the seven agents. Its centrepiece is the System Health line across all 23 rounds, with the two lowest points marked. A user can click any point on the line to read what happened that round, and a round slider moves the whole application to that moment.

title: Project Proposal
text:
6.2 Vital Signs, the four causes
This screen opens the health score into its four meters, each on its own tab. Pressure shows the market sentiment and stock signal feeding the strain. Transparency shows the mix of open, private, and covert channels. Decision-making shows who was steering out of their role, with a chart of who held the public voice in the calm weeks against the crisis day. Accountability shows whether the Judge met its three duties, with a heatmap of where the oversight failed.

title: Project Proposal
section: 6.3 Network, who was at the centre
text:
6.3 Network, who was at the centre
A reply network of the seven agents, with node size and colour carrying the centrality measures, a switch between measures, and a time window to compare the calm weeks against the crisis day.

title: Project Proposal
section: 6.4 Sandbox, what would have changed it
text:
6.4 Sandbox, what would have changed it
A replay of the breach chain, step by step, with a panel of interventions the user can switch on and off while the health at the breach hour recomputes from the underlying data.

title: Project Proposal
text:
6.5 Verdict, the conclusion
The closing screen lays out the three competing explanations, a deliberate leak, an oversight breakdown, and a compounding chain, and walks through the evidence for and against each before landing on the reading the data supports.
